name: Infostealer with RAT Deployment on Developer Workstation (T1555)

pg-c2-blocked.description: C2 domains/IPs blocked at all egress points

pg-secrets-rotated.description: Developer secrets (SSH keys, signing keys, vault tokens) rotated

pg-code-repos-clean.description: Source code repositories verified clean (no backdoors injected)

pg-cicd-verified.description: CI/CD pipelines and build artifacts verified

proc-root.name: Infostealer & RAT Incident Response
proc-root.notes: End-to-end response for infostealer execution on developer workstation with second-stage RAT deployment and C2 beaconing. Covers TTP hunting, C2 takedown, credential rotation, privilege assessment, supply chain verification, and full workstation rebuild.

proc-detect.name: Detection
proc-detect.notes: Detect infostealer execution via EDR behavioral alerts (credential store access, browser data harvesting) and C2 beaconing patterns from second-stage RAT.

proc-detect-edr.name: EDR Behavioral Detection
proc-detect-edr.notes: EDR detects suspicious process chain: user-initiated download -> execution of unsigned/low-reputation binary -> child processes accessing credential stores (Login Data, cookies.sqlite, Windows Credential Manager, SSH key files).

proc-detect-c2-beacon.name: Detect C2 Beaconing
proc-detect-c2-beacon.notes: Network detection identifies periodic beaconing pattern (regular intervals with jitter) to suspicious domains/IPs. May use HTTPS with domain fronting or DNS tunneling for covert channel.

proc-detect-credential-access.name: Detect Credential Store Access
proc-detect-credential-access.notes: Alert on unauthorized access to browser credential databases (Login Data, Cookies, Web Data), Windows Credential Manager (vaultcmd), SSH private keys (~/.ssh/), and application tokens (~/.config/, environment variables).

proc-analysis.name: Analysis & Scoping
proc-analysis.notes: Determine infostealer family, identify all exfiltrated credential types, analyze RAT capabilities, map C2 infrastructure, and determine full scope of stolen data.

proc-identify-stealer.name: Identify Infostealer Family
proc-identify-stealer.notes: Analyze malware sample to identify family (RedLine, Raccoon, Vidar, Lumma, StealC, etc.). Determine capabilities: browser credentials, crypto wallets, FTP clients, email clients, Discord tokens, VPN configs, SSH keys. Submit hash to VirusTotal/MalwareBazaar for intelligence.

proc-analyze-exfil.name: Determine Exfiltrated Credentials
proc-analyze-exfil.notes: Based on stealer capabilities and workstation state, enumerate ALL potentially stolen credentials: (1) Browser saved passwords for all profiles, (2) Browser session cookies (GitHub, cloud consoles, internal tools), (3) SSH private keys (~/.ssh/), (4) Git credential helpers, (5) Cloud CLI tokens (aws, az, gcloud), (6) IDE stored credentials, (7) VPN/RDP saved credentials, (8) Windows Credential Manager entries, (9) Application-specific tokens (Slack, Teams, npm, PyPI), (10) Environment variables with secrets.

proc-analyze-rat.name: Analyze RAT Capabilities
proc-analyze-rat.notes: Reverse-engineer or identify the second-stage RAT. Determine capabilities: keylogging, screen capture, file exfiltration, remote shell, lateral movement modules, persistence mechanisms. Identify if RAT has self-propagation or credential harvesting beyond initial stealer.

proc-map-c2.name: Map C2 Infrastructure
proc-map-c2.notes: Extract all C2 indicators: domains, IPs, ports, communication protocols, beacon intervals, fallback channels. Use passive DNS, WHOIS, certificate transparency logs to map related infrastructure. Identify hosting providers for takedown requests.

proc-contain.name: Containment
proc-contain.notes: Immediate containment: isolate workstation, block C2 at all egress points, rotate all potentially compromised credentials, terminate all active sessions, revoke all tokens.

proc-isolate-workstation.name: Isolate Affected Workstation
proc-isolate-workstation.notes: Immediately network-isolate the developer workstation via EDR isolation feature or switch port shutdown. Maintain host powered on for forensic evidence preservation. Do NOT allow developer to continue using the device.

proc-block-c2-network.name: Block C2 at Network Egress
proc-block-c2-network.notes: Block all identified C2 domains and IPs at: (1) Perimeter firewall, (2) DNS resolver (sinkhole), (3) Web proxy/CASB, (4) EDR network containment rules. Apply blocks organization-wide to detect other potentially infected hosts.

proc-rotate-credentials.name: Rotate All Compromised Credentials
proc-rotate-credentials.notes: Rotate ALL credentials that were accessible from the developer workstation: (1) AD account password, (2) All browser-saved passwords (enumerate from browser profile), (3) SSH keys - generate new keypairs and remove old public keys from authorized_keys on all servers, (4) Cloud CLI credentials (aws configure, az login tokens), (5) Package registry tokens (npm, PyPI, NuGet), (6) Git credentials and GitHub PATs, (7) VPN certificates, (8) Database connection strings, (9) Any secrets in IDE configurations or .env files.

proc-terminate-sessions.name: Terminate All Active Sessions
proc-terminate-sessions.notes: Terminate ALL sessions for the affected developer account: (1) Kill all RDP/SSH sessions, (2) Purge Kerberos tickets (klist purge) on all machines the account accessed, (3) Invalidate AD refresh tokens, (4) Sign out of all browser sessions (Google, Microsoft, GitHub, etc.), (5) Terminate VPN sessions, (6) Force re-authentication on all SSO-protected applications.

proc-revoke-tokens.name: Revoke All API Tokens & OAuth Grants
proc-revoke-tokens.notes: Revoke: (1) All GitHub Personal Access Tokens, (2) GitHub OAuth app authorizations, (3) Cloud provider service principals/access keys, (4) npm/PyPI/NuGet publish tokens, (5) Slack/Teams bot tokens, (6) CI/CD service connections and pipeline tokens, (7) Any API keys accessible from the workstation.

proc-hunt.name: Threat Hunting for TTPs
proc-hunt.notes: Proactively hunt across the environment for indicators of the same infostealer/RAT on other endpoints, lateral movement from the compromised workstation, and use of stolen credentials.

proc-hunt-iocs.name: Hunt for IOCs Across Environment
proc-hunt-iocs.notes: Search all endpoints for: malware file hashes, malicious process names, suspicious registry keys, scheduled tasks matching RAT persistence, C2 beacon patterns in network logs. Use EDR retrospective search and SIEM correlation.

proc-hunt-lateral.name: Hunt for Lateral Movement
proc-hunt-lateral.notes: Search for lateral movement from the compromised workstation: (1) RDP connections to other hosts, (2) SMB/WinRM sessions initiated, (3) SSH connections to servers, (4) kubectl/cloud CLI commands executed against clusters, (5) Source code repository access from unusual locations.

proc-hunt-credential-use.name: Hunt for Stolen Credential Usage
proc-hunt-credential-use.notes: Search authentication logs for use of stolen credentials from unexpected sources/locations: (1) GitHub audit log for PAT usage, (2) Cloud provider CloudTrail/Activity Log for API key usage, (3) VPN authentication from unusual geolocations, (4) Package registry publish events, (5) CI/CD pipeline modifications.

proc-hunt-other-endpoints.name: Hunt for Infostealer on Other Endpoints
proc-hunt-other-endpoints.notes: Sweep all developer workstations and build servers for same infostealer family. Check if initial infection vector (e.g., fake tool download, compromised package, malicious ad) may have affected others on the team.

proc-takedown.name: C2 & Malicious Infrastructure Takedown
proc-takedown.notes: Request takedown of malicious C2 infrastructure and distribution sites to protect other potential victims and prevent attacker from maintaining access.

proc-takedown-hosting.name: Request Hosting Provider Takedown
proc-takedown-hosting.notes: Submit abuse reports to hosting providers (AWS, Azure, DigitalOcean, bulletproof hosters) hosting C2 servers. Include evidence of malicious activity, IOCs, and request immediate suspension.

proc-takedown-domain.name: Request Domain Registrar Takedown
proc-takedown-domain.notes: Submit abuse complaints to domain registrars for C2 domains. Request suspension/sinkholing. Engage CERT/national authority for expedited takedowns if registrar is unresponsive.

proc-takedown-distribution.name: Request Malware Distribution Site Takedown
proc-takedown-distribution.notes: Identify and request takedown of the site/platform from which the developer downloaded the infostealer (compromised website, fake tool site, malicious ad landing page, poisoned search result). Report to Google Safe Browsing, Microsoft SmartScreen, and relevant platform.

proc-share-iocs.name: Share IOCs with Community & CERT
proc-share-iocs.notes: Share indicators with: (1) Industry ISAC, (2) National CERT (e.g., CERT-Bund/BSI), (3) Threat intelligence platforms (MISP, OTX), (4) Vendor threat feeds (EDR vendor, firewall vendor). Publish anonymized IOCs if appropriate.

proc-privilege-assessment.name: Privilege & Access Assessment
proc-privilege-assessment.notes: Comprehensively assess all privileges and access rights of the compromised developer account to determine blast radius and potential impact of stolen credentials.

proc-assess-ad-groups.name: Assess AD Group Memberships
proc-assess-ad-groups.notes: Enumerate all AD group memberships (direct and nested) of the affected account. Identify: admin groups, privileged groups (Server Operators, Backup Operators), application-specific groups granting elevated access. Determine if account had local admin on any servers.

proc-assess-cloud-access.name: Assess Cloud & SaaS Access
proc-assess-cloud-access.notes: Determine cloud access: (1) Azure/AWS/GCP IAM roles and permissions, (2) Azure AD/Entra ID roles, (3) Access to production vs. dev subscriptions, (4) Kubernetes cluster access (RBAC roles), (5) SaaS admin roles (GitHub org owner, Jira admin, etc.).

proc-assess-code-access.name: Assess Source Code & CI/CD Access
proc-assess-code-access.notes: Determine: (1) Which repositories the developer has write access to, (2) Branch protection bypass capabilities, (3) CI/CD pipeline admin access, (4) Access to deployment secrets/variables, (5) Package publishing rights (npm, PyPI, Docker Hub), (6) Code signing key access.

proc-assess-production-access.name: Assess Production System Access
proc-assess-production-access.notes: Determine direct production access: (1) SSH access to production servers, (2) Database admin access, (3) Kubernetes production namespace access, (4) Access to customer data stores, (5) Jump host / bastion host access. This determines if customer data may be at risk.

proc-eradicate.name: Eradication
proc-eradicate.notes: Fully eradicate malware from the environment, verify no supply chain compromise occurred, and confirm no attacker persistence remains.

proc-wipe-workstation.name: Wipe & Reimage Workstation
proc-wipe-workstation.notes: Full disk wipe and reimage of the affected developer workstation from a clean gold image. Do NOT attempt to clean in-place - the RAT may have additional persistence mechanisms not identified. Preserve forensic disk image before wiping.

proc-verify-repos.name: Verify Source Code Repository Integrity
proc-verify-repos.notes: Audit all repositories the developer had write access to: (1) Review all commits since infection date for unauthorized changes (backdoors, dependency modifications, CI config changes), (2) Check for force-pushes that may have altered history, (3) Verify no malicious GitHub Actions/workflows were added, (4) Check for unauthorized branch protection rule changes.

proc-verify-cicd.name: Verify CI/CD Pipeline Integrity
proc-verify-cicd.notes: Audit CI/CD pipelines: (1) Review pipeline definitions for unauthorized modifications, (2) Check build artifact integrity (compare hashes), (3) Verify no malicious steps were injected, (4) Check for unauthorized service connections or variable modifications, (5) Review deployment history for suspicious deployments during exposure window.

proc-verify-published-packages.name: Verify Published Package Integrity
proc-verify-published-packages.notes: If developer had package publishing rights: (1) Check npm/PyPI/NuGet publish history for unauthorized versions, (2) Compare published artifacts against known-good source, (3) If compromise suspected, yank affected versions and publish security advisory.

proc-recover.name: Recovery & Rework
proc-recover.notes: Rebuild developer environment with hardened security posture. Implement architectural improvements to prevent recurrence: application allowlisting, credential isolation, reduced developer privileges.

proc-rebuild-workstation.name: Rebuild Developer Workstation
proc-rebuild-workstation.notes: Provision clean workstation with: hardened SOE, updated EDR with strict policy, application allowlisting for unsigned executables, browser with no password save (use enterprise password manager), credential guard enabled.

proc-harden-dev-env.name: Harden Developer Environment
proc-harden-dev-env.notes: Implement: (1) Container-based or VM-based development environments for untrusted code, (2) Disable browser password saving org-wide (enforce password manager), (3) Short-lived credentials via credential broker, (4) Hardware security keys for MFA, (5) Restrict admin rights on developer workstations.

proc-reduce-privileges.name: Implement Least Privilege
proc-reduce-privileges.notes: Review and reduce developer account privileges: (1) Remove unnecessary AD group memberships, (2) Implement JIT (Just-In-Time) access for production, (3) Separate development and production credentials, (4) Remove direct production database access, (5) Implement break-glass procedures instead of standing access.

proc-implement-credential-isolation.name: Implement Credential Isolation
proc-implement-credential-isolation.notes: Deploy: (1) Windows Credential Guard to prevent LSASS credential theft, (2) Remote Credential Guard for RDP (prevents credential caching on remote hosts), (3) Short-lived SSH certificates instead of static keys, (4) Ephemeral cloud credentials via federation (no long-lived access keys), (5) Workload identity for CI/CD (no static service account credentials).

proc-lessons.name: Lessons Learned
proc-lessons.notes: Post-incident review: assess infection vector (social engineering, search poisoning, fake tool), evaluate EDR detection gaps, review developer privilege model, assess credential storage practices, evaluate supply chain security controls, determine if security awareness training updates needed.

ref-002.name: MITRE ATT&CK - Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates

bo-001.scope: All credentials and sessions accessible from developer workstations
bo-001.purpose: Ensure stolen credentials cannot be used for unauthorized access
bo-001.context: Infostealers harvest ALL accessible credentials making the blast radius proportional to developer privilege level

bo-002.scope: Source code, CI/CD pipelines, and published software packages
bo-002.purpose: Verify and maintain integrity of the software supply chain
bo-002.context: Compromised developer credentials can lead to supply chain attacks affecting downstream customers

bo-003.scope: Attacker C2 infrastructure and malware distribution sites
bo-003.purpose: Disrupt attacker operations and protect other potential victims
bo-003.context: Takedown of C2 and distribution infrastructure limits attacker capability and reduces community risk

smg-001.context: Speed of containment directly correlates with blast radius limitation
smg-001.scope: All compromised credentials and active sessions
smg-001.purpose: Measure time from detection to complete credential invalidation
smg-001.focus: Mean time to contain (MTTC) for credential-based compromises
smg-001.criteria: All credentials rotated and sessions terminated within 2 hours of detection

smg-002.context: Unauthorized code changes can propagate to customers before detection
smg-002.scope: All repositories and packages accessible by compromised account
smg-002.purpose: Verify no supply chain compromise occurred during exposure window
smg-002.focus: Completeness and speed of supply chain integrity verification
smg-002.criteria: All accessible repos and packages audited within 24 hours; no unauthorized changes confirmed

sm-001.description: Time from initial EDR alert to complete credential rotation and session termination
sm-001.goal: Minimize the window during which stolen credentials remain valid
sm-001.method: Track time from first alert to last credential rotated and last session killed
sm-001.interpretation: Under 2 hours excellent; 2-6 hours acceptable; over 6 hours indicates containment procedure failure
sm-001.reporting: Post-incident timeline; security operations KPI dashboard

sm-002.description: Percentage of accessible repositories and packages verified clean within 24 hours
sm-002.goal: Ensure no supply chain compromise went undetected
sm-002.method: Track audit completion of all repos/packages the compromised account had write access to
sm-002.interpretation: 100% is required; anything less indicates residual supply chain risk
sm-002.reporting: Eradication phase status updates; post-incident report

bm-001.description: Elapsed minutes from first detection alert to completion of all credential rotations and session terminations

bm-002.description: Count of repositories and packages verified clean within 24 hours

bm-003.description: Total count of repositories and packages the compromised account had write access to
